speaker: interviewee
text: So, this man, he had red hair, Jewish looking, big fellow. All the Sonderkommando men were big fellows. And I think he gave somebody-- a guard-- salami or something. They, they smuggled all kinds of things-- provisions-- into the camp, the Sonderkommando. And he gave me some food. And it was organized with no difficulty at all, and I was sleeping in the bunk next to him in the Sonderkommando block.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I moved from 11-- no, I moved from 13 to 11. And they went off, and he says, what shall I bring you? Just like that, he says, what shall I bring you? So, I said, well, you know, anything to eat. And he brought salami and bread, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this block was a terrible place to be, as well. I mean, the whole place was terrible to be. But these men came back completely-- first of all, they were tired, exhausted, because it was a very heavy work. And secondly, they came back distraught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you about their work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. They were distraught because every three months, there was a selection of the Sonderkommando. So they were being weeded out, you know, slowly, so they didn't survive. Galen had a number, an Auschwitz number, like 17 or something. He had been there, sort of from the beginning, and he was a foreman of one of the groups, crematorium number three. Significant because that's the crematorium that blew up. And the men would cry. The men would literally cry. They were so-- you know, exhaustion and-- and yet they were-- they sort of behaved callously because, you know all this--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Very well. It was like, doing something for this boy was, was something special because this was a child in their midst, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: You being the boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And there were several of us like that. They, they didn't just take me out. There were several boys like that. I was there about two or three days, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every night, Galen would bring me something. He brought me a pair of wonderful skiing boots. I had them throughout the time. Which some child had taken off, and he smuggled them back into the camp-- wonderful boots. My boots were-- my shoes that I had from home were already completely worn out, and they were pinching me. He did a lot for me. I mean, he fed me for a start and then boots was the next important thing. I mean, I, I still had my clothes, but they were all small on me because I was growing as well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, I told you about his lecture on homosexuality. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 1 SECOND] nothing went on, but [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] a couple of nights later, Galen tells me, your mother's dead. I spoke to her. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, there were something like 4,000 people, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] you know, to be gassed. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] The old that were left-- no, perhaps less, perhaps 2,000 because the men and the women went off. But, it was a multitude. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't believe him. And I asked myself, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] why is he telling me this? I mean, I know she's dead. You know, I don't need a sort of actual statement that he met her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I concluded that he wanted me to cry, and he wanted me to regard him as a sort of intimate pal. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] And this thing flashed through-- you know, this lecture-- flashed through-- flashed through my mind that I mustn't get intimate with this man. And next morning before he left, and they-- they sort of left at daybreak. And they worked long into the night, not an eight hour day or something. And they worked shifts anyway, so they had overlapping shifts. I said, you've got to help me get out of here. And I was told to go out into the camp and find myself a job as a runner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him-- Galen. He-- they called him Galen. Galen means redhead, redhead or redneck. He looked like a redneck. So, he says, no problem. I'll speak to the guard on the gate. And he goes to the gate, and he says, here, let him slip out. He needs to go, go in the rest of the camp and find himself a job. I still had the band which said, [NON-ENGLISH] So he said, as you go around, if anybody asks you where you're from, you say [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's no problem. He, he was-- he was a friend. And I went around department to department. I wanted to go to Kanada because I knew that was the best place. But the men in Kanada were very-- you know, they were all so callous, old lags who had been in Auschwitz 1 and saw all this beating and, and, and this real raw treatment of people. I mean, when you were in the, in the compound, you were sort of isolated a little bit because, unless your saw some instance of people being beaten, even though the crematoria were half a mile away, you didn't spend the day thinking about crematoria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I went around the departments. I get to the clothing department, and I speak to the kapo. And he's a German prisoner. I can see because he's a political. And he had this blue uniform, you know, that I described to you, Freddy. And he also had-- his left hand was lame. He, he sort of had a short left hand, like this, but he was a powerful, big man.

speaker: interviewer
text: I speak to him, and I say, I want to be [? laufer ?] in your department. I was [? laufer ?] in [NON-ENGLISH] in the familienlager, and now I have come to be in the block with the Sonderkommando. And have you got a job as a runner? And he looks at me-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] and this is all German, you see. He was a German. Looks at me, and he says, that's a good idea. I'm going to have two. Take me back to your block. And he walks back with me-- he's only next-- because the kleiderkammer was 15. This was my last department, my last resort, you know? If I wasn't going to get a job there, I don't know what-- what I would have done. So I didn't want to be at the front of the camp because I was told, keep out of Mengele's way. So, we walk in, and I wanted to find a particular friend of mine who was Yehuda Backon. But he just picked the first one, you know. The boys were milling all around. He says, I'll have him. So, the two of us went back. And he marches us straight to the tailor in the clothing department. And the tailor is a Polish Jew called Nitka.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Slavic languages, Nitka means thread. I don't even remember his surname because we all called him Nitka. But, it's really funny calling a man thread because he looked like that. He was very thin, very small, and he was a sort of little Jew, very Jewish looking. And as soon as he opened his mouth, you could tell the Yiddish accent. And he could actually talk best in Yiddish. He spoke Polish. He spoke Russian. He spoke French. I'll tell you later why he spoke French. It's really hilarious. He was quite a character, this Nitka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they start working out what sort of cloth he has got, and how he's going to dress these two boys. A tailor making us new clothes in Auschwitz. OK? People are walking around in rags. Jewish tailor. So, he takes out a bit of gray. So he says, nice trousers. And then he takes out a bit of beige, and he says, I haven't got enough for a jacket, but I'll make these short jackets. And he says, I know what. We'll dress them up as bell boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he made us an outfit as bell boys in a hotel, the two of us. And he sewed, sewed everything terribly tight because he didn't have enough clothes-- cloth. By the way, all his sewing was by hand. All the people that he had working were seams-- seam-- were other tailors. Everything was done by hand because the SS didn't allow any machinery into the camp, not even sewing machines.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, they had needles and threads, and Nitka made the-- and he had these ready in about two or three days. And we looked terrific, I mean, absolutely a picture. How were you regarded by the other prisoners in this special uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they couldn't get over it that there were kids amongst them. And they brought us-- in the kleiderkammer-- that's the clothing department-- they were also able to smuggle things because they were working with Kanada. And they were working as sorters in the Kanada department. So they were stealing stuff that was being taken to Germany, and they were trading stuff with the Polish outside workers. The big trade-- the, the sort of currency of the camp-- was vodka, because the SS could be bribed with vodka. So, they would steal diamonds and trade for usually salami and vodka. And salami and vodka was the contraband that was traded with the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to keep yourself clean?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a washing up place, block number 17, right opposite my block. The-- and next to it was a little latrine. The latrine was-- if you can imagine a block, one of these barrack blocks converted to a latrine. There was this stove in the middle. And then there were about three rows of seats on this side and three rows of seats on the other side with concrete trenches into which people did their business. And there was an underground sewage which was built for this. And next to this was a block with all one tap of water next to another, hundreds of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were-- you were able to keep your uniforms clean?

speaker: interviewee
text: What these uniforms that were being made for us? Oh, absolutely. We were spotless.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were your duties now?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a sinecure. The whole job was just a sinecure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you saying that you didn't really have any work? I had no work, really, not in the kleiderkammer. It was a sinecure because they wanted to have-- it's a good idea to have some kids amongst us. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first thing that happened was that [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] the officer who was in charge of the Kleiderkammer was in favor of this arrangement of having two runners. And he also thought up all kinds of errands that we should do. For example?

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, to go to the kitchens and ask them how many striped uniforms they need for the next issue during, during Waschetauschen. Waschetauschen, every six weeks, we were supposed to get new underwear. That was standing orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, talking about standing orders, in the kleiderkammer, people were all the time talking about standing orders. The, the German expression is [GERMAN] standig befehle. And there was standig befehle this and standig befehle that. Everything was worked out. You know, there were regulations for the camp. We never saw anything written down, but somewhere there was a book which said what the standig befehle were. I keep asking everybody, have you ever heard of standig befehle? Have you ever seen such a book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helmut Spritzer remembers the tagesbefehle, the orders of the day book. But he never remembers standig befehle. He says, there must have been standig befehle. You're quite right. But, he says, I've never seen one. So, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Waschetauschen-- one Waschetauschen we had outside the Kleiderkammer. And of course, the throng of people, they all want clean shirts, you see, so the first come first served. So, people cause a riot.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they cause a riot, the people who are handing out the shirts are supposed to go out with sticks and make order, you know, by beating people over the heads. And people were beaten into submission. And the SS as well, two or three. And if the thing would get too bad, then the SS would say, OK, you're behaving like, like animals, so we'll treat you like animals. [GERMAN], [? how ?] [? up, ?] you know? And the ground would clear. People would disappear.

speaker: interviewee
text: He'd close the lorry, and the standing orders would-- never mind the standing orders. No shirts would be handed out. You know, that's-- there must have been another standing order, like, if there's a riot, stopping the riot overrules everything else. I'm interpreting from what they did, not from seeing the standing orders. So, these kind of messages. But in the kleiderkammer, it was some-- sometimes quite interesting, because the kleiderkammer was responsible for organizing the women's details, and all the men who were in-- working in the kleiderkammer were organizing women's details to go and sort clothes in Kanada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I had messages to go to the Frauenlager, which was across the railway yards. It was a long way to go, you see? So I was always very glad to go to the Frauenlager because it was like a-- like an adventure. Were you able to go within the German quarters as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I once went into one of the guard barracks, and I saw where they kept the arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the submachine guns, you know, hanging behind the guard's room. And that was good knowledge. I was meeting Galen more or less every evening, once every day. Some sort of-- he'd have business things, and, you know, there was a scuttlebug going on in the camp. There's always something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a scuttlebug?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, arrangements. Prisoners were buying and selling. [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] It was a prison, you see? Now you go see what's going on in prisons. They are-- they apparently have unlimited drugs in prisons. How do they get them? They don't get them officially, but they get them. And no matter how much they talk in Parliament about it, they can't stop it. It was the same in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you told Galen about the arms that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and he told me all kinds of things as well. And then I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he tell you? What sort of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, later, I'll tell you later. No, he didn't tell me. He didn't really tell me secrets. He-- for example, he once told me, [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] do you know how we save fuel? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, I say, what do you mean save fuel? He says, it takes a long time to burn a body, so we put three together. We put a fat man, and a woman, and a child-- you know, the pallets that are wheeled in. It takes 15 minutes to burn, and then it's red hot, so you can't actually load the next pallet. And they had a-- he was describing that they had 12 of these crematorium ovens. They had to load them in sequence, and they save fuel by burning human fat, because the people who came. And he said, the worst thing is when we are burning bodies of people who die, because they are so thin that the bones won't burn. When we switch off, the bones are left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The kinds of stories-- he, he was relieving his own-- his own trauma and his own fears. But he would trade, you know, vodka for salami, or clothes. There was a sort of interchange of goods going on all the time. Then one of the-- the men were terribly-- the men in the kleiderkammer were really lucky, because they had access to women, and they were-- they f-- women and men fell on each other when they could get together. You know, like, tomorrow we die, so we have to have sex. And these men had-- had access.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have access as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they tried to talk me into selecting a girlfriend, and on one of these outings, you know, when we went to sort out clothes, there were 1,000 women, I don't know, from France standing there. Have you ever seen 1,000 naked women? It's not a very sexy picture. And this [GERMAN], his name was Herting-- I lived with Herting and Nitka in the same-- in a sort of office. Really privileged quarters we had. Herting was a Belgian. And they would bring back food, and I would sort of get it-- get it ready in the evening. Herting always showed me how to make improvised Belgian dishes. Like, for example, I made pancakes. He taught me how to make pancakes. He taught me how to make a fondue with cheeses. He brought a whole lot of cheeses. He says, look, the thing to do with these cheeses is to make a fondue. So, I made a fondue, and he told me exactly what to do. And it was delicious.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, Herting says, look, you can have any of these girls. And all I have to do is give them some clothes and they'll do anything for you. And I just couldn't-- perhaps I wasn't mature enough. I just-- I just couldn't. It, it, it was-- and Nitka goes along and chooses a young girl. This is something that you were party to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he takes her off, and she follows him, naked, into a barrack, you know? And the prisoners had it all arranged, you know? And we come home that afternoon or evening. The women were kept naked for about five hours. It was just one of these German things. The clothes were all there. We gave them rags. But there were good clothes in store, you know, but the Germans took-- kept the stores for some future date. But they gave out rags to these women. Men as well. Nitka comes back. He says, I love this girl. He-- Nitka was from Paris. He would say, [YIDDISH], in Yiddish, I am a Frenchman. He so didn't look like a Frenchman, you see. And he spoke French with such a Yiddish accent, it sounded like Yiddish. And he says, I know I am a married man-- I'm touching the mic. I shouldn't be doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know I am a married man, and I'm not supposed to, but I mean, what is man supposed to do? We're going to die tomorrow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I love this girl, she's wonderful. What a body, and this. I felt really sexless at this, this sort of approach to lovemaking or sex, in fact. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, if we get freed-- like, we knew the Russians were coming-- if we get freed, and if my wife survives, I'll adopt her as my daughter. And if my wife doesn't survive, I'll marry her. So, I thought to myself, that will be the day, but didn't say anything. Rudy was another fellow there. And Nitka was going to make him a uniform, and he was going to escape. We were all planning for Rudy to escape in a uniform. The way this was done was that every time an SS officer came into the kleiderkammer, Nitka would offer to sew for him a uniform. And they really had-- some of them had uniforms made. And Nitka would always made-- make them very tight. He, anyway, was a tight tailor. He, he-- somehow, everything was tight-fitting with Nitka. And he made these uniforms, and he would always have a bit of cloth. So, if you would have two bits of cloth, he says-- he would show me. You know, he'd have them sort of underneath all the other [NON-ENGLISH] that he had stored. And there would be German uniform cloth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- he'd say, look, here I have the back, and here I have the two fronts, you see, for the jacket. And I've got one sleeve. I need another sleeve, you see? The next uniform, I'll get the sleeve. So then I'll have the jacket. And then comes the trousers, so then we're there. Then we've got the hat to do. The hat will be the most complicated because of the insignia. So, that was Nitka's thing. It was like, at some time in the future, Rudy would escape. Meanwhile, Rudy would get hold of girls and go to them. So, Rudy says to the two of us, come, I'll talk to my girlfriend, and she'll find girlfriends for you two. So, we are in the mannerlager. How will you talk to your girlfriend? So, he says, oh, if we go behind, she'll-- she's in the C, C camp. And they're all Hungarian girls there, and they'll come to the wire. And I'll tell my girlfriend to bring some young girls for you-- for you two. So, sure enough, he says whom to call, and the girl comes, and they are talking across two wires and a connecting rod. And they're calling to each other, you know, this and that-- We're going to have to stop here. [LAUGHTER]
